keywords.description: let - Creates a new variable binding. By default variables are immutable. Use 'let mut' to make them changeable.
mut - Makes a variable mutable so you can change its value after creation. Essential for variables that need to be modified.
fn - Defines a function that can be called to execute code. Functions help organize code and enable reuse.
pub - Makes items (functions, structs, modules) accessible from outside their current module. Controls code visibility and API design.
struct - Creates a custom data type by grouping related fields together. The foundation of data modeling in Rust.
keywords.example: struct Person { name: String, age: u32 }

keywords.description: enum - Defines a type that can be one of several different variants. Perfect for representing choices or states.
impl - Adds methods and functionality to structs and enums. This is how you define behavior for your custom types.
keywords.example: impl Person { fn greet(&self) { println!("Hi, I'm {}", self.name); } }

keywords.description: trait - Defines shared behavior that different types can implement. Like interfaces in other languages but more powerful.
if - Executes code conditionally based on a boolean expression. The cornerstone of program logic and decision making.
match - Compares a value against patterns and executes corresponding code. More powerful than switch statements - ensures all cases are handled.
keywords.example: match result { Ok(value) => use_value(value), Err(e) => handle_error(e) }

keywords.description: for - Iterates over collections, ranges, or anything that implements Iterator. The most common way to loop in Rust.
while - Repeats code as long as a condition remains true. Perfect for processing until a goal is reached.
loop - Creates an infinite loop that runs until you explicitly break out of it. Useful for event loops and continuous processing.
break - Immediately exits the current loop, skipping any remaining iterations. Use to stop looping when a condition is met.
keywords.example: for x in data { if x == target { break; } }

keywords.description: continue - Skips the rest of the current loop iteration and jumps to the next one. Use to skip processing certain items.
keywords.example: for x in data { if x < 0 { continue; } process(x); }

keywords.description: return - Immediately exits a function and optionally returns a value to the caller.
keywords.example: fn divide(a: f64, b: f64) -> Option<f64> { if b == 0.0 { return None; } Some(a / b) }

keywords.description: use - Imports items from other modules into the current scope, avoiding repetitive module paths.
keywords.example: use std::collections::HashMap; // Now you can use HashMap directly

keywords.description: mod - Organizes code into modules for better structure and namespace management. Helps keep large projects organized.
async - Makes a function asynchronous, returning a Future that can be awaited. Essential for concurrent programming.
await - Waits for an async operation to complete, yielding control to other tasks while waiting.
static - Creates a global variable that exists for the entire program lifetime. Use sparingly for truly global state.
const - Declares a constant value that's computed at compile time and never changes.
else - Provides alternative code to execute when the if condition is false.
self - Refers to the current module or the instance of a struct in methods.
Self - A type alias for the current type. Makes code cleaner in impl blocks.
where - Adds constraints to generic types, making complex type bounds more readable.
keywords.example: fn process<T>(item: T) where T: Clone + Debug { /* */ }

keywords.description: unsafe - Allows operations that bypass Rust's safety guarantees. Use only when absolutely necessary.
extern - Declares functions from other languages (like C) or specifies calling conventions.
crate - Refers to the root of the current compilation unit. Use to access items from your crate's root module.
super - Refers to the parent module in the module hierarchy. Use to access items one level up.
as - Performs explicit type conversion between compatible types.
in - Specifies what to iterate over in for loops.
type - Creates a type alias to give a new name to an existing type.
move - Forces a closure to take ownership of all captured variables instead of borrowing them.
ref - Creates a reference to a value in pattern matching instead of moving it.
dyn - Indicates a trait object for dynamic dispatch at runtime.
String - Stores owned, growable UTF-8 text that you can modify. Use when you need to build, modify, or own text data.
keywords.example: let mut name = String::from("Hello"); name.push_str(", World!");

keywords.description: str - References immutable text data (string slice). Use &str for reading text without owning it.
Vec - Stores a growable list of items on the heap. Use when you need a dynamic array that can change size.
Option - Represents a value that might exist (Some) or not exist (None). Use instead of null to safely handle missing values.
Result - Represents either success (Ok) or failure (Err). Use for operations that can fail and need error handling.
Some - Wraps a value inside an Option to indicate that the value exists.
None - Represents the absence of a value in an Option.
Ok - Wraps a successful value in a Result.
Err - Wraps an error value in a Result.
Box - Allocates values on the heap instead of the stack. Use for large objects, recursive data structures, or trait objects.
Arc - Enables multiple owners to share read-only data across threads safely.
Rc - Enables multiple owners to share data within a single thread.
keywords.example: let shared = Rc::new(data); let copy = shared.clone();

keywords.description: HashMap - Stores key-value pairs with fast lookups, insertions, and deletions.
HashSet - Stores unique values with fast membership testing.
u8 - Stores small unsigned integers (0 to 255). Perfect for bytes, RGB color values, or small positive counts.
u16 - Stores medium unsigned integers (0 to 65,535). Use for port numbers, Unicode code points.
u32 - Stores large unsigned integers. Use for IDs, large counts, or when you need more range than u16.
u64 - Stores very large unsigned integers. Use for file sizes, large timestamps, or very big counts.
i8 - Stores small signed integers (-128 to 127). Use when memory is tight.
i16 - Stores medium signed integers (-32,768 to 32,767). Use for coordinates, small measurements.
i32 - The default integer type for most whole numbers. Good performance and sufficient range.
i64 - Stores large signed integers. Use for timestamps, file sizes, large counts.
f32 - Stores single-precision floating point numbers. Use when memory is tight.
f64 - The default floating point type with double precision. Use for scientific calculations.
bool - Stores true or false values for logical operations.
keywords.example: let is_valid = true; if is_valid { proceed(); }

keywords.description: char - Stores a single Unicode character (4 bytes). Use for individual characters, emojis.
keywords.example: let letter: char = 'A'; let emoji: char = '🦀';

keywords.description: usize - Unsigned integer the same size as a pointer. Use for array lengths, vector indices.
isize - Integer the same size as a pointer. Use for array indexing and memory offsets.
keywords.example: let index: isize = vec.len() as isize - 1;

keywords.description: Copy - Trait for types that can be copied bit-for-bit. Enables implicit copying instead of moving.
keywords.example: #[derive(Copy, Clone)] struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }

keywords.description: Clone - Trait for explicit duplication via clone() method. More flexible than Copy.
Debug - Trait for formatting with {:?}. Essential for debugging and printing.
Display - Trait for user-facing formatting with {}. Implement for custom string representation.
keywords.example: impl Display for User { fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter) -> Result { write!(f, "User: {}", self.name) } }

keywords.description: Default - Trait providing default values. Use default() to create instances with sensible defaults.
keywords.example: #[derive(Default)] struct Config { timeout: u32, retries: u32 }

keywords.description: PartialEq - Trait for partial equality comparison using == and !=.
keywords.example: #[derive(PartialEq)] struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }

keywords.description: Eq - Trait for full equality. Requires PartialEq and indicates reflexive equality.
PartialOrd - Trait for partial ordering comparison. Enables <, <=, >, >= operators.
Ord - Trait for total ordering. Enables sorting and use in BTreeMap keys.
Hash - Trait for hashing. Required for HashMap keys and HashSet elements.
Iterator - Trait for types that can be iterated over. Provides map, filter, collect methods.
keywords.example: impl Iterator for Counter { type Item = u32; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> { /* */ } }

keywords.description: IntoIterator - Trait for types convertible to iterators. Enables for loops.
keywords.example: impl IntoIterator for MyCollection { type Item = T; type IntoIter = MyIter; /* */ }

keywords.description: From - Trait for infallible type conversions. Provides into() method automatically.
keywords.example: impl From<u32> for MyType { fn from(value: u32) -> Self { MyType(value) } }

keywords.description: Into - Trait for consuming conversions. Usually get this automatically from From implementation.
keywords.example: fn process<T: Into<String>>(input: T) { let s = input.into(); }

keywords.description: TryFrom - Trait for fallible type conversions that can fail.
keywords.example: impl TryFrom<i32> for NonNegative { type Error = &'static str; fn try_from(value: i32) -> Result<Self, Self::Error> { /* */ } }

keywords.description: TryInto - Trait for fallible consuming conversions. Get automatically from TryFrom.
AsRef - Trait for cheap reference-to-reference conversions.
keywords.example: fn process<P: AsRef<Path>>(path: P) { let path = path.as_ref(); }

keywords.description: AsMut - Trait for cheap mutable reference conversions.
keywords.example: fn modify<T: AsMut<[u8]>>(mut data: T) { let bytes = data.as_mut(); }

keywords.description: Deref - Trait for immutable dereferencing operations. Enables automatic dereferencing.
keywords.example: impl Deref for MyBox<T> { type Target = T; fn deref(&self) -> &T { &self.0 } }

keywords.description: DerefMut - Trait for mutable dereferencing operations.
keywords.example: impl DerefMut for MyBox<T> { fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut T { &mut self.0 } }

keywords.description: Drop - Trait for custom cleanup when value goes out of scope. Like destructors.
keywords.example: impl Drop for FileHandle { fn drop(&mut self) { println!("Closing file"); } }

keywords.description: Send - Marker trait for types safe to transfer between threads.
keywords.example: fn spawn_thread<T: Send + 'static>(data: T) { thread::spawn(move || { process(data); }); }

keywords.description: Sync - Marker trait for types safe to share references between threads.
keywords.example: static COUNTER: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0); // AtomicUsize is Sync

keywords.description: Sized - Marker trait for types with known size at compile time. Most types implement this.
keywords.example: fn generic<T: Sized>(value: T) {} // T must have known size

keywords.description: Unpin - Marker trait indicating it's safe to move after pinning.
keywords.example: async fn process<T: Future + Unpin>(fut: T) { fut.await; }

keywords.description: Future - Trait for asynchronous computations. Core of async/await system.
keywords.example: impl Future for MyFuture { type Output = Result<T, Error>; fn poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context) -> Poll<Self::Output> { /* */ } }

keywords.description: FnOnce - Trait for closures that can be called once, taking ownership of captured variables.
keywords.example: fn call_once<F: FnOnce() -> i32>(f: F) -> i32 { f() }

keywords.description: FnMut - Trait for closures that can be called multiple times and mutate captured variables.
keywords.example: fn call_mut<F: FnMut() -> i32>(mut f: F) -> i32 { f() }

keywords.description: Fn - Trait for closures that can be called multiple times without mutating captured variables.
keywords.example: fn call_fn<F: Fn() -> i32>(f: F) -> i32 { f() }

keywords.description: macro_rules! - Defines declarative macros for code generation and metaprogramming.
println! - Prints formatted text to stdout with newline. Most common output macro.
keywords.example: println!("Hello, {}! You are {} years old.", name, age);

keywords.description: print! - Prints formatted text to stdout without newline.
eprintln! - Prints formatted text to stderr with newline. Use for error messages.
eprint! - Prints formatted text to stderr without newline.
format! - Creates formatted string without printing. Returns String.
keywords.example: let message = format!("User {} has {} points", name, score);

keywords.description: panic! - Terminates program with error message. Use for unrecoverable errors.
assert! - Panics if condition is false. Use for runtime invariant checking.
keywords.example: assert!(x > 0, "x must be positive, got {}", x);

keywords.description: assert_eq! - Panics if two values are not equal. Common in tests.
assert_ne! - Panics if two values are equal. Opposite of assert_eq!.
debug_assert! - Like assert! but only enabled in debug builds. Use for expensive checks.
unreachable! - Marks code that should never execute. Panics if reached.
keywords.example: match value { 1..=10 => process(), _ => unreachable!("Value should be 1-10") }

keywords.description: unimplemented! - Placeholder for code not yet implemented. Panics when called.
keywords.example: fn complex_algorithm() -> Result<i32, Error> { unimplemented!("TODO: implement algorithm") }

keywords.description: todo! - Indicates code that needs to be completed. More explicit than unimplemented!.
keywords.example: fn calculate_tax(income: f64) -> f64 { todo!("Implement tax calculation") }

keywords.description: vec! - Creates Vec with initial elements. Convenient vector creation.
keywords.example: let numbers = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5]; let zeros = vec![0; 100];

keywords.description: include! - Includes contents of file as code at compile time.
include_str! - Includes file contents as string literal at compile time.
include_bytes! - Includes file contents as byte array at compile time.
env! - Gets environment variable value at compile time.
option_env! - Gets optional environment variable at compile time.
cfg - Conditional compilation based on target or features.
derive - Automatically implements traits for structs and enums.
keywords.example: #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)] struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }

keywords.description: allow - Suppresses compiler warnings for specific code.
warn - Forces compiler warnings for specific code patterns.
deny - Turns warnings into compilation errors.
forbid - Forbids certain code patterns, cannot be overridden.
test - Marks function as unit test. Run with cargo test.
bench - Marks function as benchmark test for performance measurement.
ignore - Skips test unless specifically requested with --ignored.
should_panic - Test passes only if function panics.
inline - Suggests compiler to inline function calls for performance.
keywords.example: #[inline] fn fast_math(x: f64) -> f64 { x * x }

keywords.description: repr - Controls memory representation of structs and enums.
keywords.example: #[repr(C)] struct CStruct { x: i32, y: i32 }

keywords.description: no_mangle - Prevents name mangling for FFI functions.
keywords.example: #[no_mangle] pub extern "C" fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { a + b }

keywords.description: Path - Represents filesystem paths in a platform-agnostic way.
PathBuf - Owned, mutable path. Like String but for filesystem paths.
keywords.example: let mut path = PathBuf::from("/home"); path.push("user"); path.push("file.txt");

keywords.description: File - Represents an open file handle for reading/writing.
BufReader - Buffered reading for improved I/O performance.
BufWriter - Buffered writing for improved I/O performance.
Mutex - Mutual exclusion primitive for thread-safe data sharing.
keywords.example: let counter = Arc::new(Mutex::new(0)); let guard = counter.lock().unwrap();

keywords.description: RwLock - Reader-writer lock allowing multiple readers or one writer.
keywords.example: let data = RwLock::new(vec![1, 2, 3]); let reader = data.read().unwrap();

keywords.description: mpsc - Multi-producer, single-consumer channels for thread communication.
keywords.example: let (tx, rx) = mpsc::channel(); tx.send(42).unwrap(); let value = rx.recv().unwrap();

keywords.description: thread - Thread creation and management utilities.
keywords.example: let handle = thread::spawn(|| { println!("Hello from thread!"); }); handle.join().unwrap();

keywords.description: AtomicUsize - Lock-free atomic usize for concurrent programming.
keywords.example: static COUNTER: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0); COUNTER.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);

keywords.description: Duration - Represents a span of time for timeouts and delays.
Instant - Represents a moment in time for measuring elapsed time.
keywords.example: let start = Instant::now(); do_work(); println!("Elapsed: {:?}", start.elapsed());

keywords.description: SystemTime - Represents system time that can go backwards.
keywords.example: let now = SystemTime::now(); let since_epoch = now.duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).unwrap();

keywords.description: BTreeMap - Sorted map implemented as B-tree. Keys must implement Ord.
BTreeSet - Sorted set implemented as B-tree. Values must implement Ord.
keywords.example: let mut set = BTreeSet::new(); set.insert(42); set.insert(17);

keywords.description: HashSet - Hash-based set for fast membership testing. Values must implement Hash.
keywords.example: let mut set = HashSet::new(); set.insert("item"); if set.contains("item") { /* */ }

keywords.description: VecDeque - Double-ended queue, efficient insertion/removal at both ends.
keywords.example: let mut deque = VecDeque::new(); deque.push_back(1); deque.push_front(0);

keywords.description: LinkedList - Doubly-linked list for efficient insertion/removal at arbitrary positions.
keywords.example: let mut list = LinkedList::new(); list.push_back(1); list.push_front(0);

keywords.description: BinaryHeap - Priority queue implemented as binary heap. Max-heap by default.
keywords.example: let mut heap = BinaryHeap::new(); heap.push(3); heap.push(1); heap.push(4);

keywords.description: Cow - Clone-on-write smart pointer for efficient handling of borrowed/owned data.
keywords.example: fn process_string(s: Cow<str>) -> Cow<str> { if s.contains(' ') { s.replace(' ', "_").into() } else { s } }

keywords.description: Cell - Provides interior mutability for Copy types in single-threaded contexts.
keywords.example: let cell = Cell::new(42); cell.set(100); let value = cell.get();

keywords.description: RefCell - Provides interior mutability with runtime borrow checking.
Pin - Ensures pointed-to value cannot be moved, important for async/await.
keywords.example: fn process_pinned(value: Pin<&mut MyStruct>) { /* value cannot be moved */ }

keywords.description: ManuallyDrop - Wrapper to prevent automatic dropping of values.
keywords.example: let wrapped = ManuallyDrop::new(expensive_resource); // Won't drop automatically

keywords.description: MaybeUninit - Wrapper for potentially uninitialized data.
keywords.example: let mut uninit: MaybeUninit<i32> = MaybeUninit::uninit(); unsafe { uninit.write(42); }

keywords.description: true - Boolean literal representing a true value.
false - Boolean literal representing a false value.
FromStr - Trait for types that can be parsed from a string. Powers the .parse() method.
Error - Base trait for all error types. Implement for custom error types along with Display and Debug.
Read - Trait for reading bytes from a source. Implemented by File, TcpStream, stdin, etc.
keywords.example: use std::io::Read; let mut buf = String::new(); file.read_to_string(&mut buf)?;

keywords.description: Write - Trait for writing bytes to a destination. Implemented by File, TcpStream, stdout, etc.
dbg! - Debug-prints an expression's value along with file/line info. Returns the value for inline use.
concat! - Concatenates literals into a static string at compile time.
stringify! - Converts a token tree to a string literal at compile time.
matches! - Returns true if an expression matches a pattern. Concise alternative to match.
keywords.example: if matches!(value, Some(x) if x > 5) { handle(); }

keywords.description: Stderr - A handle to the standard error stream.
keywords.example: use std::io; let mut stderr = io::stderr();

keywords.description: Stdin - A handle to the standard input stream.
keywords.example: use std::io; let stdin = io::stdin(); let mut line = String::new(); stdin.read_line(&mut line)?;

keywords.description: Stdout - A handle to the standard output stream.
keywords.example: use std::io::{self, Write}; let mut stdout = io::stdout(); writeln!(stdout, "hello")?;

keywords.description: Sender - Sending half of a multi-producer, single-consumer channel.
keywords.example: use std::sync::mpsc; let (tx, rx) = mpsc::channel(); tx.send(42)?;

keywords.description: Receiver - Receiving half of a multi-producer, single-consumer channel.
keywords.example: use std::sync::mpsc; let (tx, rx) = mpsc::channel(); let val = rx.recv()?;

keywords.description: TcpListener - A TCP socket server, listening for connections.
TcpStream - A TCP connection between a local and remote socket.
Command - A process builder for spawning external commands.
Child - Representation of a running or exited child process.

snippets.description: Function template - Creates a complete function definition with parameters and return type
Struct template - Creates a custom data type with named fields
Implementation block template - Used to define methods and associated functions
Enum template - Creates a type with multiple variants
Trait template - Defines shared behavior that different types can implement
Pattern matching template - Provides exhaustive pattern matching for enums and other types
Conditional template - Creates an if statement with optional else clause
If-let pattern - Combines pattern matching with conditional execution
For loop template - Creates iteration over collections, ranges, or iterators
While loop template - Creates a loop that continues while a condition remains true
Infinite loop template - Creates a loop that runs forever until explicitly broken
Main function template - Entry point for Rust applications
Unit test template - Creates a test function with the #[test] attribute
Derive macro template - Automatically implements common traits for structs and enums
Result type template - For functions that can return errors
Option type template - For values that might not exist
Vector creation template - Creates a new empty vector
HashMap creation template - Creates a new empty hash map
Print line template - Prints formatted text to stdout with newline
String formatting template - Creates formatted strings without printing
Unwrap with default template - Extract value from Option/Result or use default
Match Result template - Handle Result with explicit pattern matching
Async function template - Creates an asynchronous function
Tokio spawn template - Spawns an async task
Tokio channel template - Creates a message passing channel
Custom error enum template - Creates a comprehensive error type
Builder pattern template - Creates a builder struct for configuration
Iterator chain template - Common iterator operations chained together
Closure template - Creates a closure with explicit types
Thread spawn template - Spawns a new thread
Mutex template - Thread-safe shared data
File reading template - Read entire file contents
File writing template - Write content to file
Serde serialization template - Add serde traits to struct
JSON parsing template - Parse JSON from string
HTTP client template - Make HTTP request with reqwest
Async main function template - Entry point for async applications
Test module template - Create a test module with common test functions
Benchmark template - Create a benchmark function
Lazy static template - Create a lazily initialized static variable
OnceCell template - Thread-safe lazy initialization
Regex template - Create and use regular expressions
Clap CLI template - Command line argument parsing
Derive macro template - Create custom derive macros
Procedural macro template - Create function-like procedural macros
Attribute macro template - Create attribute procedural macros
Custom error type template - Create comprehensive error handling
Builder pattern template - Fluent API for object construction
State machine template - Type-safe state transitions
Actor pattern template - Message-based concurrency
WebSocket client template - Real-time communication
Database connection template - SQL database operations
Web server template - HTTP server with routing
Async stream template - Processing data streams asynchronously
Configuration management template - Environment-based configuration
Custom iterator template - Implement Iterator trait
Plugin system template - Dynamic plugin loading
Memory-mapped file template - Efficient file I/O
Parallel processing template - CPU-intensive tasks with Rayon
Implement Display trait for a type to enable format! and println! usage
Implement From trait for type conversion
Implement Error trait for a custom error type
Implement Iterator trait for a custom type
Implement Default trait with custom defaults
Implement Drop trait for custom cleanup logic
Constructor method (associated function) following Rust convention
Test module with setup — idiomatic Rust test structure
Assert two values are equal with optional message
Assert two values are not equal
Declare a mutable variable
if-let pattern matching on Option
if-let pattern matching on Result
while-let loop for consuming Options/Results
Map Result error to a different type
Read a file to string using std::fs
Write string to file using std::fs
Create and populate a HashMap
Create a Vec with initial values using vec! macro
Create an Arc<Mutex<T>> for thread-safe shared state
Spawn a thread with a cloned Arc for shared state
Create an mpsc channel
Common derive macros for structs
Serde derive macros for serialization
Public struct with fields and common derives
Public enum with variants and common derives
Parse a value from a string
Create a formatted String
Collect an iterator into a Vec
Collect an iterator of pairs into a HashMap
Chain filter and map on an iterator
Main function returning Result for the ? operator
Type alias for a complex type
Constant declaration
Static variable declaration

items.name: HashMap
items.description: Hash map implementation providing O(1) average-case lookups

items.name: HashSet
items.description: Hash set implementation for storing unique values

items.name: BTreeMap
items.description: B-tree map providing ordered key-value storage

items.name: BTreeSet
items.description: B-tree set providing ordered unique value storage

items.name: VecDeque
items.description: Double-ended queue allowing efficient push/pop at both ends

items.name: BinaryHeap
items.description: Priority queue implemented as binary heap

items.name: File
items.description: A reference to an open file on the filesystem

items.name: OpenOptions
items.description: Options for opening a file: read, write, append, create, truncate

items.name: DirEntry
items.description: An entry inside a directory, returned by read_dir iterator

items.name: FileType
items.description: Represents the type of a file (file, directory, or symlink)

items.name: Metadata
items.description: Metadata about a file: size, permissions, timestamps

items.name: ReadDir
items.description: Iterator over the entries in a directory

items.name: read_to_string
items.description: Reads the entire contents of a file into a String

items.name: read
items.description: Reads the entire contents of a file into a Vec<u8>

items.name: write
items.description: Writes a slice of bytes to a file, creating or overwriting it

items.name: create_dir_all
items.description: Creates a directory and all its parent directories recursively

items.name: remove_file
items.description: Removes a file from the filesystem

items.name: remove_dir_all
items.description: Removes a directory and all its contents recursively

items.name: copy
items.description: Copies the contents and permissions of one file to another

items.name: read_dir
items.description: Returns an iterator over the entries in a directory

items.name: metadata
items.description: Returns the metadata for a file or directory

items.name: canonicalize
items.description: Returns the canonical, absolute form of a path

items.name: exists
items.description: Returns true if the path points to an existing entity

items.name: Read
items.description: Trait for reading bytes from a source

items.name: Write
items.description: Trait for writing bytes to a destination

items.name: BufRead
items.description: Trait for reading from a buffered source, enabling read_line and lines()

items.name: Seek
items.description: Trait providing cursor-like random access to a byte stream

items.name: BufReader
items.description: Wraps a reader with a buffer for more efficient reads

items.name: BufWriter
items.description: Wraps a writer with a buffer for more efficient writes

items.name: Cursor
items.description: Wraps an in-memory buffer providing Seek + Read/Write

items.name: Error
items.description: The I/O error type returned by std::io operations

items.name: ErrorKind
items.description: Categorizes I/O errors: NotFound, PermissionDenied, etc.

items.name: stdin
items.description: Returns a handle to the standard input stream

items.name: stdout
items.description: Returns a handle to the standard output stream

items.name: stderr
items.description: Returns a handle to the standard error stream

items.name: copy
items.description: Copies all bytes from a reader to a writer

items.name: empty
items.description: Creates a reader that reads zero bytes (always returns EOF)

items.name: repeat
items.description: Creates a reader that infinitely yields a single byte

items.name: sink
items.description: Creates a writer that discards all data written to it

items.name: SeekFrom
items.description: Seek position: Start(u64), End(i64), or Current(i64)

items.name: Stdin
items.description: Handle to the standard input stream

items.name: Stdout
items.description: Handle to the standard output stream

items.name: Stderr
items.description: Handle to the standard error stream

items.name: spawn
items.description: Spawns a new thread, returning a JoinHandle for waiting on completion

items.name: sleep
items.description: Puts the current thread to sleep for the specified duration

items.name: current
items.description: Gets a handle to the current thread

items.name: park
items.description: Blocks the current thread until unparked by another thread

items.name: yield_now
items.description: Cooperatively gives up the CPU to the OS scheduler

items.name: available_parallelism
items.description: Returns the number of hardware threads available

items.name: JoinHandle
items.description: Handle for waiting on a spawned thread to finish

items.name: Thread
items.description: A handle to a thread, used for identification and unparking

items.name: Builder
items.description: Thread builder for configuring thread name and stack size

items.name: ThreadId
items.description: A unique identifier for a thread

items.name: LocalKey
items.description: Thread-local storage key created by thread_local! macro

items.name: Mutex
items.description: Mutual exclusion lock protecting shared data across threads

items.name: RwLock
items.description: Reader-writer lock allowing multiple readers or one writer

items.name: Arc
items.description: Atomically reference-counted pointer for shared ownership across threads

items.name: Barrier
items.description: Synchronization point that blocks until all threads arrive

items.name: Condvar
items.description: Condition variable for blocking/waking threads on a condition

items.name: Once
items.description: One-time initialization primitive, guaranteeing code runs at most once

items.name: OnceLock
items.description: A cell that can be written to only once and then read safely

items.name: MutexGuard
items.description: RAII guard returned by Mutex::lock(), releases lock on drop

items.name: RwLockReadGuard
items.description: RAII guard for a read lock on an RwLock

items.name: RwLockWriteGuard
items.description: RAII guard for a write lock on an RwLock

items.name: PoisonError
items.description: Error returned when a lock is poisoned (panicked while held)

items.name: WaitTimeoutResult
items.description: Result from Condvar::wait_timeout, indicates if timeout expired

items.name: size_of
items.description: Get the size of a type in bytes

items.name: size_of_val
items.description: Get the size of a value in bytes

items.name: align_of
items.description: Get the alignment of a type

items.name: forget
items.description: Forget a value without calling its destructor

items.name: replace
items.description: Replace a value and return the old one

items.name: take
items.description: Take a value, leaving Default::default() in its place

items.name: from_raw_parts
items.description: Create slice from raw pointer and length (unsafe)

items.name: from_raw_parts_mut
items.description: Create mutable slice from raw pointer and length (unsafe)

items.name: PhantomData
items.description: Zero-sized type for phantom type parameters

items.name: Sized
items.description: Marker trait for compile-time known size

items.name: Unpin
items.description: Marker trait for types safe to move after pinning

items.name: min
items.description: Return the minimum of two values

items.name: max
items.description: Return the maximum of two values

items.name: Hash
items.description: Hashable trait for use in hash maps

items.name: ToOwned
items.description: Trait for converting borrowed data to owned

items.name: RefCell
items.description: Interior mutability with runtime borrow checking

items.name: OnceCell
items.description: Cell that can be written to only once

items.name: Rc
items.description: Reference-counted smart pointer for single-threaded use

items.name: Pin
items.description: Pointer that prevents moving of the pointed-to value

items.name: Poll
items.description: Represents whether async computation is ready

items.name: Waker
items.description: Handle for waking up async tasks

items.name: catch_unwind
items.description: Catch panics and convert to Result

items.name: _mm_add_ps
items.description: SIMD add operation for packed floats

items.name: _mm256_add_ps
items.description: AVX SIMD add operation for packed floats

items.name: Vec
items.description: A growable, heap-allocated array. The most commonly used collection in Rust.

items.name: IntoIter
items.description: An iterator that moves elements out of a Vec

items.name: Drain
items.description: A draining iterator that removes and yields elements from a Vec range

items.name: Splice
items.description: A splicing iterator that replaces a range with another iterator

items.name: String
items.description: A growable, heap-allocated UTF-8 string. The owned counterpart of &str.

items.name: FromUtf8Error
items.description: Error when converting bytes to String fails due to invalid UTF-8

items.name: FromUtf16Error
items.description: Error when converting UTF-16 to String fails

items.name: ToString
items.description: Trait for converting a value to a String. Auto-implemented for Display types.

items.name: Drain
items.description: A draining iterator over the characters of a String

items.name: Option
items.description: Represents an optional value: Some(T) or None. Rust's null-safe alternative.

items.name: IntoIter
items.description: An iterator over the value in Some, yielding 0 or 1 elements

items.name: Iter
items.description: An iterator over a reference to the contained value

items.name: Result
items.description: Result of an operation that may fail: Ok(T) or Err(E). Core error handling type.

items.name: IntoIter
items.description: An iterator over the value in Ok, yielding 0 or 1 elements

items.name: Iterator
items.description: The core iteration trait. Provides map, filter, fold, collect, and 70+ other combinators.

items.name: IntoIterator
items.description: Conversion into an Iterator. Required for use in for loops.

items.name: FromIterator
items.description: Creates a value from an iterator. Powers the .collect() method.

items.name: Extend
items.description: Extends a collection with the contents of an iterator.

items.name: once
items.description: Creates an iterator yielding exactly one element

items.name: empty
items.description: Creates an iterator yielding zero elements

items.name: repeat
items.description: Creates an infinite iterator repeating a value

items.name: repeat_with
items.description: Creates an infinite iterator from a closure called on each iteration

items.name: from_fn
items.description: Creates an iterator from a closure returning Option<T>

items.name: successors
items.description: Creates an iterator from a seed and a successor function

items.name: zip
items.description: Zips two iterators into a single iterator of pairs

items.name: Chain
items.description: Iterator adapter that chains two iterators sequentially

items.name: Enumerate
items.description: Iterator adapter that yields (index, value) pairs

items.name: Filter
items.description: Iterator adapter that filters elements by a predicate

items.name: Map
items.description: Iterator adapter that transforms each element with a closure

items.name: Peekable
items.description: Iterator wrapper that supports peeking at the next element without consuming

items.name: Rev
items.description: Iterator adapter that reverses the iteration order

items.name: Flatten
items.description: Iterator adapter that flattens nested iterators

items.name: FlatMap
items.description: Iterator adapter combining map and flatten

items.name: Take
items.description: Iterator adapter that yields at most n elements

items.name: Skip
items.description: Iterator adapter that skips the first n elements

items.name: Sum
items.description: Trait for types that can be summed from an iterator

items.name: Product
items.description: Trait for types that can be multiplied together from an iterator

items.name: DoubleEndedIterator
items.description: Iterator that can yield elements from both ends

items.name: ExactSizeIterator
items.description: Iterator that knows its exact length

items.name: Debug
items.description: Format using {:?}. Can typically be derived with #[derive(Debug)].

items.description: Configuration for formatting, passed to fmt methods

items.description: Precompiled format string arguments, created by format_args!

items.description: Format integers in uppercase hexadecimal using {:X}

items.description: Iterator over the components of a path as OsStr slices

items.description: The primary path separator for the current platform (/ on Unix, \\ on Windows)
